P1 Energy.question: State the equation linking energy transferred, power and time. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Energy transferred = power × time (1 mark)

P1 Energy.question: Define kinetic energy. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Energy of movement (1 mark)
Depends on mass and speed (1 mark)

P1 Energy.question: Explain the principle of conservation of energy. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Energy cannot be created or destroyed (1 mark)
It can only be transferred between stores (1 mark)

P1 Energy.question: Describe what is meant by efficiency. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Useful energy output divided by total energy input (1 mark)
Can be multiplied by 100 to give a percentage (1 mark)

P1 Energy.question: Explain how insulation reduces energy transfer in a house. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: It reduces heat transfer to the surroundings (1 mark)
Less energy is wasted (1 mark)
So the house is more energy efficient (1 mark)

P1 Energy.question: Describe one advantage of a renewable energy resource. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: It is replaced naturally (1 mark)
It will not run out / less polluting (1 mark)

P1 Energy.question: Explain one disadvantage of a non-renewable energy resource. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: It is finite (1 mark)
It will eventually run out / may produce greenhouse gases (1 mark)

P1 Energy.question: Compare renewable and non-renewable energy resources. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Renewable resources will be replenished whereas non-renewable resources are finite (1 mark)
Burning fossil fuels produces greenhouse gases (1 mark)
Some renewables are less reliable because output can vary (1 mark)
Cost / reliability / environmental impact must be considered (1 mark)

P2 Electricity.question: State what current is. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The rate of flow of charge (1 mark)

P2 Electricity.question: State the unit of potential difference. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Volt / V (1 mark)

P2 Electricity.question: Write the equation linking charge, current and time. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Charge = current × time (1 mark)

P2 Electricity.question: Describe the difference between a series circuit and a parallel circuit. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: A series circuit has one loop whereas a parallel circuit has branches (1 mark)
In a parallel circuit components are on separate branches (1 mark)

P2 Electricity.question: State one rule for current in a series circuit. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The current is the same at every point (1 mark)

P2 Electricity.question: Explain what happens to resistance when the temperature of a metal conductor increases. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Resistance increases (1 mark)
Because ions vibrate more and oppose the flow of electrons (1 mark)

P2 Electricity.question: Describe how an ammeter and a voltmeter are connected in a circuit. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: An ammeter is connected in series (1 mark)
A voltmeter is connected in parallel (1 mark)
The voltmeter is placed across the component being measured (1 mark)

P2 Electricity.question: Explain why adding bulbs in series makes them dimmer. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Total resistance increases (1 mark)
Current in the circuit decreases (1 mark)
Less energy is transferred by each bulb each second (1 mark)
So each bulb is less bright (1 mark)

P3 Particle Model of Matter.question: State the equation for density. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Density = mass ÷ volume (1 mark)

P3 Particle Model of Matter.question: Describe the arrangement of particles in a solid. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Particles are close together (1 mark)
Particles vibrate about fixed positions (1 mark)

P3 Particle Model of Matter.question: Describe one difference between particles in a liquid and particles in a gas. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Gas particles are further apart (1 mark)
Gas particles move more freely / faster (1 mark)

P3 Particle Model of Matter.question: Explain what is meant by internal energy. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: The total energy stored in the particles of a system (1 mark)
Includes both kinetic and potential energy of the particles (1 mark)

P3 Particle Model of Matter.question: State what happens to temperature during a change of state. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Temperature stays constant while the substance changes state (1 mark)

P3 Particle Model of Matter.question: Explain why heating a gas in a sealed container increases the pressure. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Gas particles gain kinetic energy (1 mark)
They move faster (1 mark)
They collide with the walls of the container more often / with greater force (1 mark)

P3 Particle Model of Matter.question: Describe specific heat capacity. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Energy needed to raise the temperature of 1 kg of a substance by 1°C (1 mark)
Units are J/kg°C / J kg^-1 °C^-1 (1 mark)

P3 Particle Model of Matter.question: Explain why a substance needs energy to melt. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Energy is transferred to the substance (1 mark)
This increases the internal energy (1 mark)
For melting the energy is used to overcome forces between particles (1 mark)
So the particles can move past each other as a liquid (1 mark)

P4 Atomic Structure.question: State what is meant by a radioactive substance. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A substance with unstable nuclei that emit radiation (1 mark)

P4 Atomic Structure.question: Name the three main types of nuclear radiation. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Alpha (1 mark)
Beta (1 mark)
Gamma (1 mark)

P4 Atomic Structure.question: Describe one property of alpha radiation. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: It is strongly ionising (1 mark)
It has a short range / is stopped by paper (1 mark)

P4 Atomic Structure.question: Explain the difference between contamination and irradiation. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Contamination is when radioactive material gets onto or into an object (1 mark)
Irradiation is exposure to radiation without the object becoming radioactive (1 mark)

P4 Atomic Structure.question: State what is meant by half-life. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The time taken for the count rate or amount of a radioactive substance to fall to half its original value (1 mark)

P4 Atomic Structure.question: Describe one use of radiation in medicine. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Gamma rays can kill cancer cells / radioactive tracers can be used in diagnosis (1 mark)
Linked medical purpose (1 mark)

P4 Atomic Structure.question: Explain why radioactive decay is described as random. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: You cannot predict exactly when a particular nucleus will decay (1 mark)
But you can predict the overall pattern for a large number of nuclei (1 mark)

P4 Atomic Structure.question: Describe how shielding can reduce the count rate from a radioactive source. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Different materials absorb radiation (1 mark)
Alpha is stopped by paper / beta by aluminium / gamma by thick lead or concrete (1 mark)
As more shielding is added less radiation reaches the detector (1 mark)
So the count rate falls (1 mark)

P5 Forces.question: State the unit of force. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Newton / N (1 mark)

P5 Forces.question: Write the equation linking force, mass and acceleration. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Force = mass × acceleration (1 mark)

P5 Forces.question: Explain the difference between mass and weight. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Mass is the amount of matter in an object (1 mark)
Weight is the force acting on an object due to gravity (1 mark)

P5 Forces.question: State what is meant by resultant force. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The overall force acting on an object after all forces are combined (1 mark)

P5 Forces.question: Describe what happens when the resultant force on an object is zero. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: The object remains at rest or continues at constant velocity (1 mark)
There is no acceleration (1 mark)

P5 Forces.question: Explain what causes thinking distance to increase. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Driver tiredness / alcohol / drugs / distractions (1 mark)
The driver takes longer to react (1 mark)

P5 Forces.question: Describe what is meant by momentum. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Momentum = mass × velocity (1 mark)
It is a measure of the motion of an object (1 mark)

P5 Forces.question: Explain how a seat belt reduces injury in a crash. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: It increases the time taken for the passenger to stop (1 mark)
This reduces the rate of change of momentum (1 mark)
So the force acting on the passenger is reduced (1 mark)
This reduces the chance of serious injury (1 mark)

P6 Waves.question: State what is meant by wavelength. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The distance between two corresponding points on adjacent waves (1 mark)

P6 Waves.question: State what is meant by frequency. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The number of complete waves passing a point each second (1 mark)

P6 Waves.question: Write the equation linking wave speed, frequency and wavelength. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Wave speed = frequency × wavelength (1 mark)

P6 Waves.question: Describe the difference between a transverse wave and a longitudinal wave. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: In a transverse wave the oscillations are perpendicular to the direction of travel (1 mark)
In a longitudinal wave the oscillations are parallel to the direction of travel (1 mark)

P6 Waves.question: State one part of the electromagnetic spectrum used for cooking food. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Microwaves (1 mark)

P6 Waves.question: Explain why infra-red radiation is useful in heaters. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Infra-red radiation transfers energy by radiation (1 mark)
It is absorbed by surfaces and warms them (1 mark)

P6 Waves.question: Describe what happens when a wave is refracted. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: The wave changes direction (1 mark)
Because it changes speed when it enters a different medium (1 mark)
Its wavelength also changes (1 mark)

P6 Waves.question: Explain why ultrasound can be used for medical scans. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Ultrasound waves reflect at boundaries between different tissues (1 mark)
The reflected waves are detected (1 mark)
A computer uses the echoes to build up an image (1 mark)
Ultrasound is non-ionising so it is safer than X-rays for some uses (1 mark)

P7 Magnetism and Electromagnetism.question: State one property of a permanent magnet. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Produces its own magnetic field / remains magnetic without an external field (1 mark)

P7 Magnetism and Electromagnetism.question: Describe the shape of a magnetic field around a bar magnet. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Field lines go from the north pole to the south pole (1 mark)
The field is strongest near the poles / field lines curve around the magnet (1 mark)

P7 Magnetism and Electromagnetism.question: Explain what an induced magnet is. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: A material that becomes magnetic when placed in a magnetic field (1 mark)
It loses most or all magnetism when removed from the field (1 mark)

P7 Magnetism and Electromagnetism.question: Describe how an electromagnet is made. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: A wire is wrapped into a coil / solenoid (1 mark)
Current passes through the coil (1 mark)

P7 Magnetism and Electromagnetism.question: State two ways to increase the strength of an electromagnet. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Increase the current (1 mark)
Add more turns to the coil / use an iron core (1 mark)

P7 Magnetism and Electromagnetism.question: Explain the motor effect. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: A force acts on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field (1 mark)
The magnetic field around the wire interacts with the external magnetic field (1 mark)
This causes the wire / conductor to move (1 mark)

P7 Magnetism and Electromagnetism.question: Describe one use of an electromagnet. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Used in scrapyard cranes / relays / electric bells / loudspeakers (1 mark)
Linked explanation of how magnetism helps the device work (1 mark)

P7 Magnetism and Electromagnetism.question: Explain why an iron core makes an electromagnet stronger. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Iron is a magnetic material (1 mark)
The iron becomes magnetised when current flows in the coil (1 mark)
This adds to the magnetic field produced by the coil (1 mark)
So the overall electromagnet is stronger (1 mark)
